food_name: Chinese Rojak (Fruit and Vegetable Salad with Fermented Prawn Paste and Peanut Sauce)
food_subgroup: Mixed salad of fresh fruit, vegetables, and fried dough in fermented prawn paste and peanut sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Chinese Rojak. Closest parent is a fruit-vegetable salad with sauce (FOODON:03315763 — mixed salad). No exact URI assigned as no direct match exists.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed salad — composite dish with vegetables, fruit, and sauce; closest FoodEx2 parent under composite dishes / mixed salads. Code A0FJP used as closest defensible parent for mixed vegetable-fruit salad with dressing.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Chinese Rojak with fermented prawn paste sauce. A0FJP (mixed salad) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Chinese Rojak or equivalent Singaporean hawker salad dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chinese Rojak weighs approximately 350g, comprising roughly 250g of mixed fresh fruit, vegetables, and you tiao, 80g of hei ko sauce, and 20g of crushed roasted peanuts.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Rojak — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chinese Rojak. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. All per-100g values are taken from the verified HPB record.

gi_source.title: Composite GI estimate for Chinese Rojak based on component GI values — pineapple (GI ~59), jicama/bangkuang (GI ~46), you tiao/fried dough (GI ~75), palm sugar (GI ~35); weighted composite GI ~58 (medium)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (www.glycemicindex.com) — component GI values; composite estimate documented per Veritas methodology
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese Rojak as a composite dish. Component GI values sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database: pineapple GI ~59 (medium), jicama GI ~46 (low), fried dough/you tiao GI ~75 (high), palm sugar GI ~35 (low). Weighted composite GI estimated at ~58 (medium) based on approximate ingredient proportions. Glycemic load per 350g serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (58 × 49g) / 100 ≈ 28.4. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium from hei ko (fermented prawn paste) sauce — approximately 480mg per 100g, equating to approximately 1,680mg sodium per standard 350g plate
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Chinese Rojak: a single plate delivers approximately 1,680mg sodium — approximately 84% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000mg — almost entirely from the hei ko sauce, and sauce reduction or substitution is the only meaningful strategy to make this dish suitable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium composite GI (~58) and moderate glycemic load (~28 GL per 350g serving); fibre from jicama and vegetables moderates glucose absorption but you tiao and palm sugar elevate glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Chinese Rojak in moderation: the medium GI (~58) and high dietary fibre (2.5g/100g) from jicama and fresh vegetables help moderate postprandial glucose, but the glycemic load of ~28 per plate is significant and portion control is essential, particularly limiting you tiao intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content (480mg/100g; ~1,680mg per plate) combined with moderate fat from peanuts and you tiao; saturated fat is relatively low at 1.0g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Chinese Rojak due to its very high sodium load (~1,680mg per plate), which significantly elevates cardiovascular risk; the saturated fat content is modest (1.0g/100g) and the fresh fruit and vegetable components provide beneficial fibre and antioxidants, but the hei ko sauce sodium burden is the dominant clinical concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory components (vitamin C from pineapple and cucumber, bromelain from pineapple, vitamin E from peanuts) offset by high sodium from hei ko sauce
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chinese Rojak provides anti-inflammatory micronutrients including vitamin C (15mg/100g) and bromelain from pineapple, but the high sodium content from hei ko sauce may exacerbate fluid retention and systemic inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis patients; sauce reduction is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Rojak (罗惹, Luó Rě) is one of Singapore's most iconic and distinctive hawker dishes, with Hokkien-Teochew origins. It is a vibrant mixed salad comprising fresh cucumber, jicama (bangkuang / turnip), pineapple chunks, bean sprouts, tau pok (tofu puffs), and pieces of you tiao (fried dough fritters), all tossed together in a pungently flavoured black sauce made from hei ko (fermented shrimp paste), palm sugar, lime juice, tamarind, and chilli, then finished with crushed roasted peanuts and sesame seeds. The result is a collision of textures — crunchy fresh vegetables, chewy tofu puffs, and crispy you tiao — and a complex flavour profile of sweet, sour, pungent, umami, and savoury notes that is quintessentially Singaporean. Chinese Rojak is distinct from Indian Rojak (which uses fried fritters in a peanut sauce) and from Malay Rojak (which uses a different sauce base). Nutritionally, Chinese Rojak provides 115 kcal per 100g, with 3.5g protein, 5.5g fat, and 14g carbohydrate. The critical clinical insight is that despite appearing to be a healthy salad, a standard 350g plate contains approximately 1,680mg sodium — nearly 84% of the WHO daily sodium limit — derived almost entirely from the hei ko sauce. The fresh ingredients themselves are nutritious, providing dietary fibre (2.5g/100g), vitamin C (15mg/100g), and potassium (180mg/100g). This dish is an important patient counselling example where the main ingredients are healthy but the condiment fundamentally transforms the nutritional profile. The composite glycemic index is approximately 58 (medium), with a glycemic load of approximately 28 per plate. It is not halal due to the fermented prawn paste and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It is dairy-free and a common hawker centre side dish or light meal in Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. The HPB record is an exact match for Chinese Rojak. GI is estimated from component values using the University of Sydney GI Database, with confidence 0.9. All mandatory multilingual names are present for a Singaporean dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Chinese Rojak composite dish not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this composite dish. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible — no significant vitamin D source in this dish per HPB). All other nutrient values are as reported by HPB Singapore Foodo. GI is an analogous composite estimate (~58, medium) based on University of Sydney GI Database component values for pineapple, jicama, you tiao, and palm sugar; no direct GI measurement for Chinese Rojak as a composite dish exists in the literature. Glycemic load per 350g serving = (58 × 49g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 28.4. The dish is not halal due to fermented prawn paste (hei ko). FoodEx2 code A0FJP used as closest defensible parent (mixed salad / composite dish); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Chinese Rojak.
